preamble: The emergence of the voiced alveolar affricate /dz/ from a phonologically empty position, often in intervocalic contexts, is a documented phenomenon in some languages, typically arising from epenthesis. This process involves the insertion of a consonant to break up a hiatus or fill an empty onset position.
phoneticEffects: Epenthesis, Fortition (as the inserted sound is a stop component of an affricate, which can be seen as a strengthening of a boundary or hiatus)

examples.note: Intervocalic insertion of /dz/ between a vowel-final verb stem and a suffix, filling a phonologically empty position (hiatus).

sources: Blevins, J. (2008). Consonant Epenthesis: Natural and Unnatural Histories. In J. Good (Ed.), Linguistic Universals and Language Change (pp. 79–107). Oxford University Press.
